lesson.menu_desc: Used to and would for past habits — how things used to be: I used to work on call, but now we have a rotation.
lesson.subtitle: Aula 17 — Comparing Then and Now (used to / would)
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Daniela Feitoza | Aula 17 -- Comparing Then and Now

listenings.text: Hi Daniela, it is Sarah, your English coach. Today, listen to one short voice note about comparing the past and the present. When you talk about a habit or a situation that was true in the past but is not true now, you use used to. The form is easy. Use used to, plus the base verb. I used to work on call every weekend. We used to fix everything by hand. For repeated past actions and routines, you can also use would, plus the base verb. Every night, the old system would crash, and we would restart it. But be careful. Would works only for repeated actions, not for past states. For a state, say I used to have an old laptop, never I would have. To make the contrast clear, add a present sentence with but now. I used to work on call, but now we have a rotation. We used to do it manually, but now it is automated. So, use used to for past habits and states, would for repeated past actions, and but now for the contrast. Talk soon.

Hello Daniela, this is Marcus. I am leaving you a quick voicemail before the all hands meeting about how the team has changed. So, here is the story. Two years ago, things used to be very different. We used to firefight every single night. The legacy system would crash, and one person would fix it alone, on call. The process used to be slow and tedious, with a huge backlog. We used to deploy everything manually, and the handover between shifts used to be a mess. But now, everything has changed. We have a proper rotation, so no one burns out. We streamlined the process and automated the deployment. We even did a full overhaul of the old code. The outdated scripts are gone. In the meeting, I want you to tell the team how we used to work and how we work now. It is a great story. Call me back when you can. Thanks, Daniela. Talk soon.
